sentences.text: My name is Jenny Lerner, J-E-N-N-Y L-E-R-N-E-R.
Yes, I did.
Eugenia Lewicka.
E-U-G-E-N-I-A L-E-W-I-C-K-A.
No.
August 21, 1912.
Now? 84 and 3 months.
And what city were you born in?
Luck, was spelled L-U-C-K.
And the country?
The country was switching from one to another.
When I was born, it was Russia.
When I was going to school, it was Poland.
What was it like?
It was a city surrounded with a river by name Svyr, S-V-Y-R, Svyr.
And I was born by the bottom of the mountain, man-made mountain, which was a fortress in the medieval ages.
When I was born, it was 36,000.
I mean, when I remember, when going to school.
There were 18,000.
The whole city consisted of 36,000.
That time.
All kinds manual work.
My father-- my father was a construction-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a carpenter.
And then he became in construction.
And he was hired by the city, and state, and other government to build their [INAUDIBLE] places.
Lazer Lerner.
Chaia Leah Feldman.
A homemaker.
We were six of us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The two were the last ones, we were twins, unidentical, opposite.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was a tomboy.
I was disobedient.
And I got a lot of beating from my father, belts on me because I was not listening.
But he loved me just the same.
He loved me very much because I was very good in school.
I was-- what would you call it-- gifted.
All then-- all of us were six, altogether.
We what?
Yes.
We-- Father built the house.
We lived in the back of the house.
In the front, we had a tenant.
It was a big kitchen, and a-- and a big room.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And a small room.
Hard as could be.
She would get up very early.
She would have to milk the cow, except for Shabbat when she had a neighborly gentile to milk the cow.
She was very orthodox, which I am not.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she sewed our dresses.
She would work maybe 16 hours.
I don't know how many.
She hardly had time to sleep, churned the butter, sewed dresses for us, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] do everything in the house.
Very strict.
She was orthodox.
We didn't cook.
We had a kind a type of an oven that you put Friday at noontime all that cholent, and whatnot, and kugel.
And she lit.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you would open noontime next day.
We-- in a way, we celebrated all of them.
Oh yes, we had a Seder.
Father was sitting at the head of the table, and we were around the table.
And we had wine and bracha.
Not the wine, it was vodka, small, small portions.
They were very charitable people.
My mother would bake starting Thursday.
We had a big table in the entry before you went into the kitchen.
And on that table was a lot of everything mother baked.
And people would come around, and they would give it to them.
Yes.
At first, I went to-- the closest was by the Russian church, a Ukrainaian school.
They had only-- by the time I got there, they were having five grades only because Poland started to close up the schools.
So when I came, they had five grades.
Next year, they would have four grades, because then from the five Ukrainian school grades, I went to the Polish, to the sixth grade.
It was hard on me because Polish was different than Ukrainian.
With mother, Yiddish.
With father, Russian.
Made my-- I remember my first mistakes.
In Poland-- in English, you don't know that.
In Polish, for instance, [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] [SPEAKING POLISH], meaning, I have bread.
I don't have bread.
But you see, it changes because of the grammar.
You don't have grammar here, so it's not a big deal.
After sixth grade, in, in town, we had four high schools.
The Russians had their own, all private.
The Russians had their own high school.
The Ukrainians had their own high school.
And with that, every school had their own uniforms and berets.
I was going-- I had no choice.
I wouldn't be accepted to the government school.
Was a no-no.
So there was a private-- Jewish private school in Polish language.
And that's where I went.
After seventh grade, I went to the Jewish high school.
True.
Yes.
There was more.
We had in school these real antisemites.
And what they do, they made the imitation, like it was one by name [?
Mieska, ?] meaning a snake.
And she was a snake.
She said, you will say, [NON-ENGLISH] And you will say, [NON-ENGLISH] And together, it becomes like a mixture.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that what it was.
In elementary school, the, the principal-- it was a girls school.
And the principal was very understanding.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She wouldn't allow all this.
And then we had to go.
I belonged to the choir.
The teacher was not discriminating.
And when it came, the birthday day for the bishop, we went to sing for his birthday.
And there, they had these young priests, clerics, they called them.
And they were walking around with chocolate boxes and treats.
And then the bishop started to [INAUDIBLE], to bless us.
So everybody kneeled.
And I wouldn't that time.
I wouldn't kneel.
So I and another girl were standing up.
Nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then there is a Polish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every-- in Poland, every day has another saint's name, every day.
There was a saint by name [INAUDIBLE] Holy Koska-- I don't know-- Koska something.
And on this day, they took us to the city theater.
We were to sing.
We rehearsed together on Fridays afternoon with the boys who came from the boys school, to prepare.
When we came to the theater, we sang our part.
We went down to the audience.
The teacher said, you wait here.
And when it's finished, we'll go back to school.
When I was sitting there, came a cleric and said, you leave.
You don't belong here.
I looked at him, and I didn't move.
I stayed right there.
I am a rebel in my lifetime.
[LAUGHS] I ignored him, and I just kept sitting because the teacher said you go back to school.
All along, it was a lot of it.
We were nine.
You see, we went-- we were in the Ukrainian school.
When we went to the Polish school, there were-- there were Czecho-- Czechish girl, Jewish girl.
We were like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what I would call it-- black sheep.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we stand-- we did stand our ground, anyway.
But we kept together, the group.
Because the rest of them were different, not exactly accepted.
Yes, when you float in this atmosphere, you have to be just ignoring them or resist them.
I didn't bring my pictures.
To the private school.
Terrible, because we paid-- let's call it dollars-- $60 a month.
I were a-- I was a twin, so we had two of us. $60 for 12 months a year.
It's only 10 months' schooling, but you pay for the summertime also.
But then I don't know how they handled their finances.
They-- this principal demanded a 13th month.
And if you didn't bring the money, they would send you out unless you bring the money.
So that's what we went through.
Very much so.
Yes.
The oldest-- depends what time of the year.
She married when she was maybe 22 or 23.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she had oldest three, I think-- two or three stillborn children, miscarriages or stillborn.
Then she had the one.
And I remember when-- you're not going to believe those years.
A baby was maybe eight or nine months when he died.
It came, and I didn't understand.
It came a Jewish man who brought a black jacket, just about like you wear.
And he wrapped that baby in it and took it like this and walked away with him.
And I couldn't understand it.
It hurt me so much.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then one of those was a living boy.
When they started, the soviets first started to bomb our city, and we had a big call it field, or meadow, belonged to us.
And they would cut the grass and put haystacks.
And when they started the bombing, my oldest sister and her child, and a neighborly Russian woman hid in the haystack.
And when they started to bomb, shrapnels fell and they tore off that clavicle of my sister.
And she died there.
The neighbor, it ripped off that part.
On the way to the hospital, she died.
And the boy, nothing happened to the boy.
Yes.
They cried day and night as to what happened, I remember.
Then when we were 14, we were going to high school, the two of us.
And it broke out scarlet fever.
My sister was the first to get it.
And she was so sick.
I remember when I woke up, my mother was walking and doing like this and crying.
She was in, they called it, crisis.
But she survived.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then when it broke on the scarlet fever, and the doctor said, now, they can be in one bed.
That means I'm on this side of the bed, and you are in this side of the bed.
You had my-- and I remember by then, we're allowed to eat.
So mother made [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] cream wheat, cream of wheat.
And she had her bowl, and I had mine.
And I quickly ate up mine, and I grabbed for hers.
And she wouldn't let me.
So we all fell out of bed.
[LAUGHS]
Then they allowed us to eat more.
And then was easier.
Those years, they didn't feed you.
Now, they feed you, regardless.
I took a course of nursing, like a Red Cross course in my home town.
But that was hardly enough.
And it was very, very-- atmosphere started to be war-like.
People sensed the war.
So they started to buy flour and things that they might need because everything disappeared from the market.
1938, I think, if I don't mislead you.
Now, on that particular Friday, 1939, September the 1st-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, wait a minute.
I didn't tell you about the sanatorium for TB children.
When the war was on, my sister and I, first on the west side of the city, I was working.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't know what to do with myself.
And I found myself-- my-- I'm confusing.
My sister went to a clairvoyance woman.
And she said, if you want, you have somebody that you're very worried about.
If you want her to survive, she has to go south of Warsaw.
If-- You have two choices.
You can go to Grojec.
It's a small town that time, only 5,000.
And she could get through the war there.
Or she could go further south to Kazimierz, a city.
But I decided, I'll better go to the one that's closer to town.
And I don't need to travel in the train when everybody recognize me.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I choose that one.
And there, I didn't know what to do with myself.
I thought I'll be a maid.
And I went to a woman who was like referring you to households.
I came there, and, and she wasn't there because her daughter was working by through the Germans in a field.
And on the way back, she said, all the way in the top of the wagon filled with hay.
And she reached out for a cherry.
And she pulled the cherry, and she fell off and broke her spine.
Therefore, the mother wasn't occupied what she used to do.
She was going crazy running around town and crying, and lost her mind or something.
In meantime, I need to be somewheres to sleep, And I was sitting all day waiting.
Finally, the-- and there was a very poor, young couple, just married.
And, and they had nothing.
And I knocked on their door.
And I said, go and check if she's back.
Go and-- she was above them, living above them.
And she didn't come.
It was very late.
And the next best, I knocked on the couple's door, and the put me between them in the bed for the night.
They had only one bed.
Yes.
Yes.
Yeah, in fact, when I was-- I think it was '38 yet.
When I-- when I was to go home by train, I had to stay all night in the train waiting.
And I was very hungry.
Next to me was sitting a peasant woman.
She had bread and milk, and she treated me.
And then she gave me her address.
And she said that I live in that place, there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And if you have no choice where to go, come, and I'll keep you.
I was walking-- by the way, I forgot to tell you.
When I was going by the train by myself, nobody was in the train because of curfew.
I was the only in the whole train.
Next to me was a package wrapped in a newspaper and tied with a string.
I touched it.
Nobody was there.
I took it.
I thought these are shoes.
I thought these are man's shoes, in fact.
When I came to my sister, my sister never went to ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was taken by a friend.
Her boyfriend was a Polish Catholic doctor.
And when she-- they were working in the same hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Her husband sent-- forgot now.
Not my twin.
And I followed what she told me to do.
Right, and this was in 1938.
You were in Warsaw.
What do you do in Warsaw?
I was working on private cases.
The doctor did refer me, were two doctors, two brother doctors.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I knew him, and he would refer me to places.
I remember one time, I went to a case.
And when I came, the patient was dead.
And she was very huge in size.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I, nevertheless, had to stay there through the night.
And in the morning, I was the one to wash her.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And because she was too big, we cut her dress in the back, and we just pulled it down, all the way in the back, cut.
That's how she was.
Women, they buried them like this, in heels, or St.
Mary's picture.
That's how I placed that picture.
I remember they gave me then to eat a sandwich with ham.
And I don't eat these things to this day.
So I threw-- I left it, and threw it in the basket and covered with paper.
Yes.
What happened to him?
One day, the war was on, and I said, Dr.-- forgot his name now-- and he had also a brother who was a dermatologist.
And I said to him, don't you see?
We're going to perish, all of us.
He said, you are young.
You'll survive.
He said, the war is like a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what do you call this?
